Walk me through the 'Forgot password' option on the login screen.

completion.content: Sure. Tap 'Forgot password' right on the login screen. The app sends a one-time passcode to your registered mobile number or email so you can verify it's you. Does resetting my app password also change my card PIN?

completion.content: No — resetting your mobile-app password leaves your card PIN untouched; the two are completely separate. The app reset just involves the 'Forgot password' flow, a one-time passcode to confirm it's you, and a new password of 8+ characters with a number and a symbol. It will, however, sign you out of your other devices.

completion.content: Open the app, head to Cards, and tap Freeze card to lock it instantly, or call the 24/7 lost-and-stolen hotline. Reporting it as stolen deactivates it permanently and automatically kicks off a replacement with a new card number, so there's no separate order to place.

completion.content: Treat it as stolen and report it right away, either by freezing it in the app (Cards, then Freeze card) or calling the round-the-clock lost-and-stolen number. Anything charged after your report won't be your responsibility, and the new card arrives in 5 to 7 business days, or 1 to 2 with the $25 rush option.

What actually triggers the international fee — is it the currency or the location of the shop?

completion.content: Either one triggers it: a purchase billed in a foreign currency, or one that's processed outside the country, gets the 2.5% fee. Spending in your local currency within the country avoids it entirely. When it does apply, the conversion is at the Visa network rate with no bank markup, listed as its own line on your statement.

Trying to budget for a Canada trip — what card fees should I plan for when paying in Canadian dollars?

completion.content: Paying in Canadian dollars means a 2.5% foreign transaction fee, since that's a foreign-currency purchase processed outside the country. The amount is converted at the Visa network rate with no extra markup from us, and it'll be itemized separately on your statement. Anything you'd pay in your home currency back home wouldn't carry the fee.
